title: Ukrainian deep-strike drones damage Su-57 stealth fighters at Shagol Airfield 1,676 km from border
summary: Satellite imagery confirmed Ukrainian drone strikes damaged multiple Su-57 fighter jets and Su-34 bombers at Russia's Shagol Airfield in Chelyabinsk Oblast, the furthest confirmed strike into Russian territory.
contents:
## Ukrainian Strike on Russia's Premier Fighter Jets

### Strike Details
The Ukrainian General Staff confirmed on May 1 that Ukrainian forces struck several Russian Su-57 fighter jets and Su-34 fighter-bombers at the Shagol Airfield in Chelyabinsk Oblast on April 25. The airfield sits approximately 1,676 kilometers from the international border — the furthest confirmed Ukrainian deep-strike action.

### Satellite Confirmation
Satellite footage posted on May 1 confirmed damage to multiple aircraft at the Shagol Airfield, corroborating Ukrainian claims.

### Technical Significance
The Su-57 Felon represents Russia's most advanced fifth-generation fighter aircraft. Damage to these aircraft at a facility considered well beyond Ukrainian strike range demonstrates:
- Dramatic expansion of Ukrainian UAV capabilities
- Vulnerability of Russian rear-area military infrastructure
- Inability of Russian air defenses to protect high-value assets deep in national territory

### Additional Airfield Strike
Ukrainian forces also struck Mi-28 and Mi-17 helicopters at an airfield near Babky, Voronezh Oblast (roughly 150 km from the frontline) on April 29.

### Range Evolution
Ukraine's Defense Ministry reported more than doubling the range of deep-strike capabilities since the 2022 invasion. The ability to reach Chelyabinsk Oblast from different approach directions complicates Russian air defense planning.

### Defense Technology Assessment
The strike represents a significant asymmetric warfare achievement, using relatively low-cost drone technology to damage Russia's most expensive and technologically advanced military aircraft.
